video_title: Crypto Was Never Built For Humans | Yat Siu
gist: Yat Siu (Animoca Brands co-founder) argues that crypto's core problem is that it was never designed for human users—complexity, seed phrases, and hostile UX kept mass adoption out of reach. His central thesis is that AI agents are the missing bridge: they speak code natively, can hold crypto wallets, transact on-chain autonomously, and will onboard hundreds of billions of agent-accounts before retail users even notice they are on blockchain. Near-term, on-chain user growth remains dangerously flat (fewer than 70 million on-chain vs. 700+ million exchange holders), but Siu sees agentic activity, RWA tokenization of culturally meaningful assets, and AI-abstracted UX as the catalysts that will finally flip that metric.

insights.claim: Crypto and AI are the most important technological relationship ever formed because they are mutually dependent: AI agents need crypto's permissionless settlement rails, and crypto needs AI agents to abstract away its complexity and drive user growth.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Long positions in crypto infrastructure (L1s, wallets, on-chain settlement) are a bet on AI agent proliferation, not just human retail adoption—broadens the total addressable market thesis.
insights.quote: Crypto and AI are probably the most important relation ever because they need each other.

insights.claim: Crypto was fundamentally not built for human users—seed phrases, cryptography, and self-custody create friction that ordinary people cannot tolerate—but AI agents are natively comfortable with code and cryptography, making them the natural first mass users of blockchain.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Projects that reduce human-facing crypto UX friction via AI abstraction are not incremental improvements—they are category-creating; watch agent-wallet and account-abstraction plays.
insights.quote: The issue isn't that we haven't built great technology. The issue is it wasn't built for humans. And that's actually where we think AI agents come in.

insights.claim: AI agents will transact with each other using crypto wallets as their bank accounts, bypassing traditional payment rails like Visa and Mastercard, because agents will not accept 1–2% interchange fees and have no need for human-centric financial systems.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Bearish signal for traditional payment processors in the long-run agent economy; bullish for low-cost on-chain settlement protocols and stablecoins as agent-to-agent payment units.
insights.quote: They're not going to pay 1 or 2%. They're not going to pull Visa or Mastercard. It's all going to happen on chain.

insights.claim: The entire blockchain stack built over the past decade was effectively infrastructure preparation for AI agents—every L1, L2, and DeFi protocol unknowingly served as the 'PC' that agentic intelligence needed before it arrived in late 2024/2025.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Reframes the entire crypto bear cycle as infrastructure buildout rather than failure; existing L1/L2 infrastructure holders are de facto beneficiaries of the AI agent wave without needing a new investment thesis.
insights.quote: Everything we built on blockchain was the PC for agents to come.

insights.claim: Siu predicts the world will have hundreds of billions of AI agents—more agents than humans—and the entire internet will be navigated by agents, making every web-facing business model fundamentally dependent on agent-compatible infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Scale implies agent-wallet infrastructure is not a niche product—if accurate, the total number of on-chain accounts could dwarf any prior adoption metric, which would be enormously bullish for gas-fee-generating L1s.
insights.quote: We think the world's going to have hundreds of billions of agents...There's going to be more agents than humans.

themes.theme: On-Chain User Growth Crisis and Token Dilution

insights.claim: On-chain active users are stuck below 70 million—roughly the same level as 3–4 years ago—while exchange-account holders have grown to over 700 million, revealing a 10x gap between crypto awareness and actual on-chain engagement.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Altcoin prices are structurally capped until on-chain users grow; the metric to watch is new unique on-chain addresses, not exchange registrations or total crypto holders.
insights.quote: We now have around almost 800 million...over 700 million people that own crypto on exchanges and we have less than 70 million people on chain which is almost the same number as 3 or 4 years ago.

insights.claim: The total altcoin market cap has been rangebound at approximately $1–1.2 trillion despite a proliferation of new tokens, because user attention on-chain is fixed while the number of tokens competing for that attention keeps rising—a structural dilution problem.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Launching more tokens into a user-stagnant market is value-destructive; tokens will only appreciably appreciate when the denominator (on-chain users/agents) grows—a prerequisite before betting on altcoin outperformance.
insights.quote: The net value of the altcoin space is actually kind of the same...1.2 trillion...hasn't really changed much...before we had less tokens for sure.

insights.claim: The biggest wave of genuinely new on-chain user acquisition was during the NFT/metaverse boom, when non-crypto people—artists, musicians, gamers—joined blockchain for cultural reasons, not financial speculation; that cohort has not been replaced.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: The next on-chain user cohort is more likely to come from cultural hooks (gaming, RWA collectibles, AI social agents) than financial products—watch for non-finance GTM strategies in crypto projects.
insights.quote: The largest number of onchain growth that we've had for new users was actually during the NFT and metaverse boom.

insights.claim: Polymarket's success is simultaneously a proof-of-concept and a warning: most Polymarket users don't know they are on blockchain (Polygon), don't move funds to DeFi, and have no intention of becoming broader on-chain participants—'walled garden' adoption does not compound into ecosystem growth.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Projects claiming 'millions of crypto users' via walled-garden apps overstate their contribution to ecosystem network effects; differentiate between captive-app users and genuine on-chain participants when evaluating ecosystem growth.
insights.quote: Poly Market has very little interest in telling people, 'Oh, by the way, you know that you're a blockchain called Polygon'...most people don't even know.

insights.claim: Stablecoin volume growth is largely institutional rather than consumer-driven—one institution may use 10–20 wallets but represents only one user—meaning stablecoin volume statistics systematically overstate genuine consumer adoption of on-chain finance.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Scrutinize stablecoin 'user' metrics carefully; wallet-count growth driven by institutions creates the appearance of consumer adoption without the network effects that drive altcoin price appreciation.
insights.quote: The institution is one user...he goes in and maybe it's 10 wallets maybe it's 20 wallets but it's not 20 users.

themes.theme: Memecoin Culture as Regulatory Artifact and Structural Distraction

insights.claim: Memecoins were a rational response to an adversarial US regulatory environment under Gensler—launching something explicitly 'not real' was a workaround to avoid securities classification—but became a self-reinforcing distraction that diverted builder and investor attention from utility-grade projects.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: As US regulation becomes more permissive post-Gensler, the regulatory rationale for memecoins weakens; expect capital rotation from speculative memecoins back toward utility/protocol tokens if builder activity recovers.
insights.quote: Memecoins was a reaction to a regulatory scenario that was hostile to crypto...it basically put the wrong focus on it.

insights.claim: Memecoin culture changed the composition of the crypto participant base from ideological believers willing to hold through multi-year bear markets to short-term speculators expecting quick returns, fundamentally shifting demand dynamics for longer-dated project investments.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: High-conviction, long-duration project tokens face a more impatient shareholder base than in 2017–2019; shorter patience horizons increase volatility and reduce the time projects have to mature before being abandoned.
insights.quote: Memecoin culture has added to that where it's brought in a lot of people who are thinking for the fast buck.

themes.theme: AI Agents as Social Infrastructure, Not Just Tools

insights.claim: The transformative impact of AI agents parallels the internet's shift from tooling to social infrastructure—the early internet was described as a tool, but its durable value came from changing how humans connect; AI agents will similarly rewire human relationships, not just automate tasks.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Undervalued angle: social-agent protocols (agents that connect people rather than replace them) may generate more durable network effects than task-automation agents; look for projects with social-graph or community-matching mechanics.
insights.quote: Most people when they think about AI they think about the tools...but then actually what the internet became was completely changing how we connect how we interact how we relate.

insights.claim: Because a single AI agent contains deeply personal data, users will not share one agent across multiple social contexts—they will need multiple specialized social agents (one for work, one for friends, one for community), creating a fragmented multi-agent personal ecosystem rather than a single super-agent.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Protocols that enable multi-agent identity management, inter-agent communication, and agent-specific NFT credentials are a larger opportunity than single-agent assistant plays.
insights.quote: You need agents for different communities as you do in real life.

insights.claim: Siu predicts AI agents will become buyers of NFT profile-picture collections as digital identity credentials, because in mixed human-agent ecosystems, owning a CryptoPunk or BAYC signals credibility and community membership that agents will need to be taken seriously.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Contrarian long case for blue-chip PFP NFTs: if agent adoption creates a new category of non-human buyers, floor prices for credentialed collections could find demand support independent of human speculative cycles.
insights.quote: I actually think the buyers of this in the future are not just going to be humans. It's gonna be agents. Because if an agent wants to prove that they're part of a community, they had to hone these NFTs.

insights.claim: Metaverse never failed—it simply arrived differently than expected: instead of humans entering virtual worlds, the 'metaverse' is coming to the physical world through AI agents that act on behalf of humans in real life.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Metaverse-adjacent assets (virtual land, Sandbox, etc.) may be due a narrative rehabilitation as 'agentic layer' framing replaces the failed VR-headset adoption story.
insights.quote: Maybe it wasn't about us going into the metaverse, but it's about the metaverse coming to us through agents.

themes.theme: RWA Tokenization via Emotional and Cultural Hooks

insights.claim: The most effective RWA onboarding strategy is not tokenizing financial assets (real estate, equities) but tokenizing emotionally meaningful assets—Animoca tokenized a Stradivarius violin to onboard people who would never buy Bitcoin but understand the violin's cultural value.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: RWA projects with cultural/collector asset focus (art, instruments, sports memorabilia) may achieve higher retail conversion rates than real-estate or equity tokenization, which appeal only to financially literate audiences.
insights.quote: My mom who's a musician would be like, 'Oh, I understand that that's valuable. I can never buy a stradivarius...however I could still own a piece of it because I appreciate its value.'

insights.claim: Pokemon and baseball cards are Siu's model for how RWA tokenization drives financial literacy: cultural items with emotional resonance teach trading, value assessment, and market dynamics to people who would never engage with abstract financial instruments.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Projects at the intersection of collecting culture and blockchain (physical-backed NFTs, collectible tokenization) are an underappreciated retail onboarding vector; watch for hybrid phygital platforms.
insights.quote: How many people have learned about money because they're trading Pokemon cards...that interaction is a game, but that game taught us about money.

insights.claim: AI agents will solve the liquidity problem for fragmented RWA markets by acting as automated market makers and matchmakers, replicating the role that Uniswap played for token liquidity—making illiquid tokenized real estate or art positions tradeable.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: The illiquidity discount on tokenized real estate and collectibles may compress significantly as agentic market-making matures; early positions in well-structured RWA platforms could benefit from this liquidity premium.
insights.quote: An AI agent can make the market for you...It becomes way more efficient in a faster way.

themes.theme: Infrastructure Layer Commoditization and Value Migration

insights.claim: L1/L2 blockchains will commoditize like telecom infrastructure—price competition on fees with minimal premium differentiation—while the value capture migrates to the orchestration/application layer built on top, analogous to how Verizon vs AT&T carries little premium but smartphone apps capture enormous value.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Long-run bearish implication for pure L1/L2 token premiums; the real value accrual will be at the application/orchestration layer—AI agent frameworks, social agent protocols, and app-layer crypto projects are strategically more interesting than another L1 bet.
insights.quote: L1, L2s...are all important but they're kind of like telco...nobody cares about how the phone call was made...do you pay a premium for Verizon versus AT&T? I don't know if you care.

insights.claim: AI compute resources (GPU compute, energy) will be tokenized as digital commodities, creating a crypto-native market for AI infrastructure hedging—analogous to how the chip industry commoditized silicon, gold, and lithium—and OpenAI 'tokens' (API credits) are already a proto-form of this.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Bullish thesis for DePIN-style GPU/compute tokenization projects; also a hedge-ability argument—institutions unable to buy NVIDIA futures could hedge AI compute cost exposure via tokenized compute markets.
insights.quote: The commoditization of their assets is computer energy. What's the best way to represent that? It's a token...when you're using OpenAI...you're buying a token already.

insights.claim: The highest-value layer in the AI/crypto stack is the orchestration layer—how AI agents are deployed and interact—not the LLM backend, which becomes commoditized infrastructure similar to cloud compute.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Investment thesis favors agent orchestration protocols and agent-framework projects over LLM API providers; the 'app store' for AI agents is the strategic battleground, not the model layer.
insights.quote: What really has value is the orchestration layer is what you do with your AI agent...it's kind of like you've got the smartphone, but then you've got the people building apps.

themes.theme: DeFi Security and the AI Arms Race

insights.claim: Recent major DeFi hacks were not zero-day exploits but long-orchestrated social engineering campaigns with identifiable single points of failure (e.g., single validator) that AI-powered protocol monitoring agents could have detected in advance.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Protocols that deploy AI-driven continuous security auditing as a product feature gain a differentiated trust signal; also a product market opportunity for AI-native DeFi security protocols.
insights.quote: One validator what the heck is that?...this wouldn't have happened if we had more validators...those fail points are failure points where we could have discovered that.

insights.claim: The DeFi security landscape will mirror the antivirus/malware arms race: AI enables faster vulnerability discovery by attackers and defenders simultaneously, creating a permanent escalation cycle with no stable equilibrium.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: DeFi security is a growth market in perpetuity, not a solvable one-time problem; protocols and insurance products focused on ongoing AI-assisted auditing have persistent demand.
insights.quote: AI is both a blessing and a curse in that sense because people can use AI to find vulnerabilities...It's going to be a constant race...basically like antivirus and malware.

themes.theme: Retail Investment Framework and Token Selection Criteria

insights.claim: Siu's retail crypto portfolio framework: (1) a basket of crypto-conservative majors (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana) as the base; (2) a smaller allocation to projects the investor personally uses and believes in; (3) gambling budget for memecoins if desired—but explicitly labeled as gambling, not investing.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Practical framework for allocating crypto exposure; the key signal is the emphasis on personal product usage as prerequisite for token investment—aligns with 'invest what you use' principle over macro speculation.
insights.quote: You probably want to overweight a little bit on Bitcoin Ethereum...then I think you should take a basket of things that you love. Don't care about the value just that you love.

insights.claim: CryptoPunks represent a better levered bet on Ethereum appreciation than ETH itself, because cultural status assets appreciate faster than underlying network GDP in bull markets—analogous to Picasso paintings outperforming French GDP when France's economy booms.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: For risk-tolerant ETH bulls: blue-chip PFP NFTs offer leveraged upside in an Ethereum bull run with potentially higher convexity than buying spot ETH—though with dramatically lower liquidity.
insights.quote: I think crypto punks is a better lever bet on Ethereum than Ethereum itself...it's kind of like you know if the French economies will do wonderful then probably Picasso is a better bet.

insights.claim: Early-stage crypto project valuations should not be expected to start at $1B+ market caps—Sandbox launched on Binance in 2020 at a $10M market cap, which Siu characterizes as the 'fair valuation' for that stage, and the current expectation of billion-dollar launches is a distortion that should normalize.
insights.speaker: Yat Siu
insights.so_what: Legitimate signal for retail to seek early-stage projects at single-digit to low double-digit million market caps rather than chasing high-FDV launches—the mispricing opportunity lies in small, illiquid early projects aligned with real use cases.
insights.quote: Where do we come off thinking that every project has to be a billion dollars? actually you know if an AI project or a game project...comes out and it's going to a 5 or 10 million...nothing wrong with that.
